--- SOURCE 1: What Are Cryptocurrency Layer 2 Scaling Solutions? ---
URL: https://coinmarketcap.com/academy/article/what-are-cryptocurrency-layer-2-scaling-solutions

SUMMARY:
Layer 2 solutions to scaling establish an additional protocol that is built on top of blockchains like those of Ethereum and Bitcoin. Like other layer 2 scaling solutions, it aims to tackle scalability problems by offloading some of the validation and transaction processing processes to another blockchain. Layer 2 scaling solutions must inherit the underlying security of the main chain. Layer 2 scalability engines and solutions likeStarkware,Optimism andArbitrum provide scaling for blockchains so that a growing number of exchanges and platforms are able to use networks like Ethereum. Ethereum’s Plasma layer 2 solution uses child or secondary blockchains that will assist the main chain in verification. StarkNet is a permissionless decentralized ZK-rollup layer 2 solution for the Ethereum blockchain.

[Blockchain](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/blockchain) technology offers tremendous benefits – [decentralization](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/decentralized), [trustless](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/trustless) interactions, high levels of security and immutable record-keeping. It has enabled a booming cryptocurrency ecosystem to develop and has underpinned consistent technological innovation. However, one of the main problems with many blockchain networks is their scalability.[Scaling problems](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/scaling-problem) are an issue when the amount of data passing through the blockchain hits a limitation due to the insufficient capacities of the blockchain.

In an ideal case, a blockchain would be able to handle an infinite number of transactions per second, also referred to as [throughput](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/throughput) or with the acronym TPS. However, the [Bitcoin](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/bitcoin/) main chain can only handle around 3-7 TPS. In comparison, Visa can process about 20,000 TPS using the centralized VisaNet electronics payment network. The difference lies in the level of decentralization and privacy that Bitcoin and other blockchains aim to provide. It takes a good deal of time and processing power to replace a simple centralized system. Each transaction must be accepted, [mined](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/mining), distributed, and validated by a global network of[nodes](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/node).

To solve these problems, blockchain developers are working to improve the scope of what a blockchain can handle. That means allowing for a higher number of transactions per second and faster processing times. One method is to use layer 2 scaling solutions. This would make the common goal of the blockchain community a reality: to make [cryptocurrencies](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-are-cryptocurrencies) and blockchain-based systems accessible to everyone in a convenient, secure and efficient manner.

Why Are Scaling Solutions Necessary?
------------------------------------

[Scaling solutions](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/scaling-solution) can tackle the aforementioned problems by providing respite for the blockchain without needing to increase[block sizes](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/block-size) or introduce other measures that would tamper with the technology’s capacity for decentralization and high levels of security.

Layer 1 Scaling Solutions
-------------------------

[Layer 1 blockchain](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/layer-1-blockchain) solutions help to improve the base protocols (E.g. [Bitcoin](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/bitcoin/)’s proof-of-work, or [PoW](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/proof-of-work-vs-proof-of-stake)) by changing how they operate as regards processing data. For example, the [Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/) network is now moving to a proof-of-stake ([PoS](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/proof-of-stake-pos)) [consensus algorithm](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/consensus-mechanism). This new method of mining supports faster transaction speeds and more efficient energy use in the mining process.

[Sharding](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/sharding) is another layer 1 scaling solution that breaks down the job of authenticating and validating transactions into smaller pieces. It spreads the workload better across the peer-to-peer ([P2P](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/peer-to-peer-p2p)) network to bring in more computing power from more nodes. This all allows for blocks to be completed faster.

However, layer 1 solutions aren’t the only avenue available to scale blockchains. [Layer 2 solutions](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/layer-2) to scaling establish an additional protocol that is built on top of blockchains like those of Ethereum and Bitcoin.

Layer 2 scaling solutions increase throughput without tampering with any of the original decentralization or security characteristics that are integral to the original blockchain.

### What Is a Sidechain?

[Sidechains](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/side-chain) are in fact something of a hybrid between layer 1 and layer 2 solutions to scaling. A sidechain is another blockchain that is linked to a main chain such as [Bitcoin](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/bitcoin/)’s. They are linked with a two-way peg (2WP) that is a protocol that allows for the open transfer of cryptocurrency from the main chain to a layer two chain that requires a degree of third-party trust.

One example of a sidechain is the[Liquid Network](https://liquid.net/), attached to Bitcoin’s main chain. Like other layer 2 scaling solutions, it aims to tackle scalability problems by offloading some of the validation and transaction processing processes to another blockchain. This frees up the main chain to take on a greater number of transactions.

### What Is a Parachain?

[Parachains](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/parachain) are short for “parallel chains.” As you can imagine, these chains run parallel to one another in a system of interconnected blockchains. They are all created within the same framework, which allows them to have the same security attributes, and they are all connected to the central relay chain. However, they can all also act independently to address their specific applications. This is the central idea behind[Polkadot](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/polkadot-new/). Parachains allow for very fast transactions as the distribution is efficiently spread to handle workloads.

### What Is Ethereum 2.0?

Ethereum 2.0 refers to the [Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/) network’s shift to a more sustainable PoS-based system that supports sharding and other scalability features. This set of improvements will increase the scalability of Ethereum and put it on par with other leading blockchains when it comes to throughput. Ethereum investors can stake their coins to earn rewards in return for their contribution to validation efforts.

### What Is a Hashgraph?

Hashgraphs leave the realm of blockchain and are considered a different technology altogether. However, they are very similar insofar as they are also a distributed ledger technology ([DLT](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/distributed-ledger-technology-dlt)). Moreover, unlike blockchain, hashgraph has been patented, and the only ledger out there that uses it is[Hedera Hashgraph](https://hedera.com/). Hedera touts itself as the third-generation of public ledger technology, following [Bitcoin](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/bitcoin/) and [Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/). It supports incredible scalability at 10,000 TPS, lower fees, less energy usage and lower processing times.

Types of Layer 2 Scaling Solutions
----------------------------------

Layer 2 scaling solutions must inherit the underlying security of the main chain. Whereas sidechains may use other networks or validators to secure the chain, layer 2 differentiates itself by inheriting its security directly from the main chain. The main layer 2 solutions are [zero-knowledge rollups](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/zero-knowledge-rollup) and[optimistic rollups](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/optimistic-rollups-for-the-rest-of-us).

Layer 2 scalability engines and solutions like[Starkware](https://starkware.co/),[Optimism](https://optimism.io/) and[Arbitrum](https://offchainlabs.com/) provide scaling for blockchains so that a growing number of exchanges and platforms are able to use networks like [Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/).

### Zero-Knowledge Rollups

Zero-knowledge rollups – aka ZK-Rollups – are bundles of data that are collateralized by a [smart contract](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/smart-contract) on the main chain while they are transported off-chain for processing and computing. They are able to produce a [block](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/block) in around a minute, with the ability to process 2,000 TPS. [Zero-knowledge](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/zero-knowledge-proof) means that all verifiers can know they have the same information without it actually being disclosed.

The ZK-rollups layer 2 scaling solution performs better than layer 1 due to the off-chain storage of data. Important data relevant to the smart contracts are requested less frequently than layer 1 blockchains. This saves large amount of processing power, and less of the blockchain capacity is used for transaction validation. Gas fees decrease as a result, making transactions faster and cheaper.

### Optimistic Rollups

[Optimistic rollups](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/optimistic-rollup) run on [Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/)’s base layer so that a huge number of smart contracts can be run without overburdening the network. They still benefit from the exact same levels of security as the Ethereum main chain. Data aggregators will compute merkle roots to achieve increased transaction speeds. However, they offer less throughput than Plasma and ZK Rollups.

The main difference compared to ZK-rollups is that layer 2 transactions take a longer time. Optimistic rollups have to rely on external validators to check the merkle roots before the state can be updated. However, the advantage is that optimistic rollups can support smart contracts in a similar way to the underlying smart contracts blockchain.

### Plasma

[Ethereum](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/ethereum/)’s Plasma layer 2 solution uses child or secondary blockchains that will assist the main chain in verification. Plasma chains are similar to smart contracts or parachains from Polkadot. However, they are structured differently in a hierarchy to take transactions from the main chain to free up work and improve salability.

Bitcoin Layer 2 Scaling Solutions
---------------------------------

### Bitcoin Lightning Network

The [Bitcoin](https://coinmarketcap.com/currencies/bitcoin/)[Lightning Network](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/lightning-network) is one of the best-known layer 2 solutions for Bitcoin. Like other layer 2 solutions, it takes transaction bundles from the main chain to be dealt with off-chain before transferring that information back. The Lightning Network also brings smart contracts to Bitcoin, which is a big improvement to the network overall.

Bitcoin lightning network promises the following benefits: instant payment, scalability, low cost and cross blockchains swaps.

As the name suggests, this layer 2 solution will introduce lightning-fast payments on the Bitcoin blockchain, as fast as milliseconds. The current [Bitcoin average transaction time](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/how-long-does-a-bitcoin-transaction-take) is about 10 minutes. However, it can vary largely if the network is congested.

The Bitcoin lightning network also claims that it is capable of processing millions to billions of TPS, which is many times higher than legacy payment providers like Visa.

By settling transactions off-chain as layer 2 solution, fees are greatly reduced, allowing for instant micropayments.

Finally, cross-chain atomic swaps can occur off-chain as long as the chains support the same [cryptographic hash function](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/cryptographic-hash-function). Bitcoin uses the [SHA-256](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/sha-256) cryptographic has function in its algorithm.

More information about the Bitcoin lightning network can be found [here](https://lightning.network/).

Ethereum Layer 2 Scaling Solutions
----------------------------------

### Starkware

Starkware is a Ethereum layer 2 scaling solution provider. It has three products: StarkNet, StarkEx and Cairo.

StarkNet is a permissionless decentralized ZK-rollup layer 2 solution for the Ethereum blockchain. Developers are now able to deploy their smart contracts permissionlessly on StarkNet's testnet. The main advantage is the ability for dApps to achieve unlimited scale, while still benefitting from Ethereum's composability and security. More information on StarkNet can be found [here](https://starkware.co/product/starknet/).

StarkEx is a layer 2 scalability engine that has been proven and deployed on mainnet since June 2020. It has been deployed in various use cases, and notable customers are DeversiFi, Immutable and dYdX. The main benefits of StarkEx is trustless scalability using ZK-STARK technology, ability to design self-custodial dApps, and robust and secure scaling solution for a wide range of uses. More information on StarkEx can be found [here](https://starkware.co/product/starkex/).

Cairo is Starkware's Turing-complete language behind both StarkNet and StarkEx. It allows for the scaling of dApps using STARKs.

Learn more in our [deep dive into Starkware](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-is-starkware-the-ultimate-guide-to-the-starkware-starknet-and-starkex-ecosystem).

### Optimism

Optimistic Ethereum is an Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) compatible optimistic rollup chain. The main benefits of deployed on Optimism is that it is fast, simple and secure. Users can move assets in and out of the network using Optimistic Ethereum Gateway, and projects looking to deploy can submit a form to get whitelisted by Optimism. Projects that meet their launch criteria will be approved within 2 weeks. On July 2021, Uniswap V3 announced its alpha launch on Optimistic Ethereum mainnet.

Learn more in our [deep dive into Optimism](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-is-optimism-the-ultimate-guide-to-the-optimism-ecosystem).

### Arbitrum

Arbitrum is a layer 2 solution designed to boost the speed and scalability of Ethereum smart contracts, while adding additional privacy features. The layer 2 platform allows developers to run unmodified EVM contracts and transactions on layer 2, without compromising on layer 1 security.

Arbitrum positions itself as the ideal scaling solution for DeFi apps, with the ability to use Arbitrum rollup to scale any Ethereum contract.

[Offchain Labs](https://offchainlabs.com/), the company behind Arbitrum, [rolled out Arbitrum One](https://offchain.medium.com/introducing-arbitrum-one-our-mainet-beta-ed0e9b63b435), their Ethereum mainnet beta on Aug. 31, 2021, and announced $120 in series B funding, valuing the firm at $1.2 billion.

Learn more in our [deep dive on Arbitrum](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-is-arbitrum).

### **Polygon**

Polygon is another layer-two solution for Ethereum, leveraging different technologies to enhance Ethereum’s scalability:

*   Polygon PoS: EVM-compatible [sidechain](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/side-chain).
*   Polygon Miden: a zk-rollup based on [Starkware](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-is-starkware-the-ultimate-guide-to-the-starkware-starknet-and-starkex-ecosystem).
*   Polygon Hermez: an open-source zero-knowledge rollup.
*   Polygon Avail: a standalone layer-2 chain with a data availability focus.
*   Polygon Zero: a zk-rollup chain.
*   Polygon Nightfall: a privacy-focused rollup chain.

In July 2022, Polygon also announced the launch of Polygon zkEVM, a zero-knowledge version of the [Ethereum Virtual Machine](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/glossary/ethereum-virtual-machine-evm). Its aim is to reduce transaction costs and improve Ethereum’s scalability before the switch to proof-of-stake.

Learn more in our deep dive into [Polygon](https://coinmarketcap.com/alexandria/article/what-is-polygon-the-ultimate-guide-to-the-polygon-ecosystem).

• Layer 2 solutions improve scalability and reduce transaction costs without compromising security.

• Off-chain processing and secondary protocols are key features that enhance performance.

Blockchain technology has revolutionized the way we think about digital value, decentralization, and trustless systems, but it faces a persistent challenge: scalability. As networks like Ethereum and Bitcoin have grown in popularity, the limitations of Layer 1 (the base blockchain) have become clear. High transaction fees and slow confirmation times have highlighted the urgent need for more scalable solutions. This is where Layer 2 blockchain solutions step in, providing a vital bridge to mass adoption and unlocking the true potential of decentralized applications.

## Understanding Layer 2 Solutions

Layer 2 solutions are protocols built on top of existing Layer 1 blockchains. Their primary goal is to improve scalability and reduce costs without compromising the security and decentralization of the underlying network. By moving most transactions "off-chain" and only settling or anchoring the results on the Layer 1 chain, Layer 2 solutions alleviate congestion and allow for significantly higher throughput.

A Guide to Layer 2 Scaling Solutions
====================================

Layer 2 scaling solutions are protocols built on top of a primary blockchain, like Ethereum, to make transactions **faster and significantly cheaper**. They act like express lanes on a congested highway, moving traffic off the main road to ease congestion and lower costs for everyone.

This approach is what makes blockchain technology practical enough for everyday use. Without it, we'd be stuck in the slow lane forever.

Why Blockchains Need Express Lanes
----------------------------------

Imagine a single-lane highway trying to handle an entire city's rush hour traffic. You’d have gridlock for hours, and anyone who managed to get through would pay ridiculously high tolls.

This is exactly the problem popular blockchains like Ethereum face. When too many people try to use the network at once, you get frustrating traffic jams (network congestion) and eye-watering tolls (gas fees).

![Image 3: Image](https://cdn.outrank.so/3af56023-0d2e-4234-a574-74a70915453f/be35ec17-d854-40b9-a0f9-c4054b914af2.jpg)
This headache is part of the "blockchain trilemma," a well-known concept that says a blockchain network struggles to be decentralized, secure, and scalable all at the same time. If you sacrifice one, you risk undermining the whole point of the network. For example, you could increase the block size to cram more transactions in, but that would make it harder for regular folks to run a node, hurting decentralization.

### The Rise of Layer 2 Scaling Solutions

This is where **Layer 2 scaling solutions** come into play. Instead of trying to rebuild the main highway (Layer 1), they build brand-new, multi-lane expressways right on top of it.

These solutions handle the bulk of transactional activity off-chain, bundling them together before sending a compressed summary back to the mainnet for final settlement. This drastically reduces the burden on the main chain.

> The core idea is simple yet powerful: move the heavy lifting to a faster, cheaper layer while inheriting the rock-solid security of the underlying blockchain. This is how Web3 applications start to feel as quick and responsive as the web apps we use every day.

By 2025, leading Layer 2 solutions like Polygon, Shibarium, and Immutable X have become the go-to platforms for scaling. They’ve slashed transaction costs to near-zero and can process transactions at incredible speeds. Naturally, this makes them a magnet for NFT marketplaces, DeFi protocols, and blockchain games that can't afford to be bogged down by mainnet congestion.

For a deeper dive, you can check out this breakdown of the [top L2 solutions and their potential](https://www.antiersolutions.com/blogs/top-10-layer-2-scaling-solutions-you-should-invest-in-by-2025/).

To get a clearer picture of the problem and the solution, let's compare the two experiences side-by-side.

### Layer 1 Bottlenecks vs Layer 2 Benefits

This table breaks down the core challenges of operating directly on a Layer 1 blockchain and shows how Layer 2s provide a much-needed fix.

| Challenge | Layer 1 (Mainnet) Experience | Layer 2 Solution |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Transaction Speed** | Slow confirmation times, especially during peak traffic. | Near-instant transaction processing. |
| **Transaction Cost** | High and unpredictable gas fees that can be prohibitive. | Extremely low fees, often costing fractions of a cent. |
| **Scalability** | Limited throughput, leading to network congestion. | High throughput, capable of handling thousands of transactions per second (TPS). |
| **User Experience** | Frustratingly slow and expensive for everyday applications. | Smooth, fast, and cheap, similar to traditional web applications. |
| **Network Security** | Inherently secure but at the cost of performance. | Inherits the security of the Layer 1 mainnet while offering superior performance. |

Ultimately, Layer 2s are not just a temporary fix; they are a fundamental evolution of blockchain architecture that makes widespread adoption possible.

The ability to analyze the flow of assets and transactions between these layers has become a crucial skill. Understanding the data is key to spotting trends and opportunities, a topic you can explore further in our guide to [blockchain data analytics](https://thecryptorecruiters.io/blockchain-data-analytics/). By offloading activity, Layer 2s solve the scalability puzzle that has held back mainstream adoption, making them absolutely essential for the future of Web3.

Understanding the Main Types of Layer 2s
----------------------------------------

Not all Layer 2 scaling solutions are created equal. Each one is designed with a specific purpose and trade-off in mind, and picking the right one is a bit like choosing a mode of transportation.

A motorcycle is fast and agile for one person, a bus is great for moving large groups, and a train operates on its own dedicated tracks. The world of Layer 2s has distinct categories that solve the scalability problem in their own unique ways.

The main categories you’ll run into are **Rollups**, **Sidechains**, and **State Channels**. Getting a handle on how these three core types work is the first step to understanding the entire Layer 2 ecosystem. Each one strikes a different balance between security, speed, and flexibility.

This diagram gives you a clear visual breakdown of these primary Layer 2 categories.

![Image 4: Image](https://cdn.outrank.so/3af56023-0d2e-4234-a574-74a70915453f/8a9496e6-cb54-40a3-b0c2-8b0bb28a4ee3.jpg)
As you can see, State Channels, Rollups, and Sidechains are all distinct branches of the bigger "Layer 2 Scaling Solutions" family, each representing a totally different architectural approach.

### Rollups: The High-Capacity Carpool

Imagine you and hundreds of other people are all trying to drive into the city center (Layer 1) during rush hour. The traffic is a nightmare. **Rollups** are like a high-capacity carpool service that gathers everyone's travel plans (transactions), bundles them into a single, efficient vehicle, and takes one trip on the main highway.

This "carpool" posts its trip details to the main chain in a highly compressed format. So, instead of hundreds of individual cars clogging up the road, there's just one. This drastically cuts down on congestion and toll costs for everyone involved.

There are two main flavors of this carpool service, which we'll dive into later:

*   **Optimistic Rollups** operate on a trust-but-verify model. They assume everyone in the carpool is honest by default but have referees who can step in and challenge any shady activity.
*   **ZK-Rollups** use some pretty advanced math (cryptography) to prove the trip was valid right from the start, giving you a higher degree of immediate certainty.

> By bundling transactions, Rollups can process **thousands of transactions per second (TPS)**—a massive jump from the limited capacity of a Layer 1 like Ethereum. This makes them a go-to choice for DeFi and other high-volume applications.

### Sidechains: The Parallel Highway System

Now, picture a completely separate, parallel highway running alongside the main one. That’s a **Sidechain**. It's an independent blockchain with its own set of traffic rules (its consensus mechanism) and its own security patrol (its validators).

This parallel highway is connected to the main one via a "bridge." You can move your car (your assets) from the main highway to this new one to take advantage of its faster speeds and lower tolls.

The key difference, though, is that the security of this parallel highway is separate from the main one. If something goes wrong on the Sidechain, the main highway's security doesn't automatically have your back. This trade-off gives developers incredible flexibility to build out entire ecosystems with custom rules, like the [Polygon](https://polygon.technology/) PoS chain, but it means users have to place their trust in the Sidechain's own security model.

### State Channels: The Private Messaging App

Finally, imagine you and a friend need to send dozens of messages back and forth really quickly. Instead of shouting every single message across a crowded room (the main blockchain), you open a private, direct-messaging channel. This is a **State Channel**.

Inside this private channel, you can exchange an almost unlimited number of messages (transactions) instantly and for next to nothing. Once you’re done with your conversation, you just close the channel, and only the final summary—like the ending balance of your accounts—gets posted to the public room.

This method is incredibly efficient but it's built for a very specific job. It's perfect for apps that have a high volume of back-and-forth interactions between a fixed number of people, like in-game micropayments or peer-to-peer data streaming. It just isn't designed for the kind of general-purpose smart contracts that a Rollup or Sidechain can handle.

By keeping these analogies in mind—the carpool, the parallel highway, and the private message—you can get a much better feel for the unique strengths and ideal uses for each type of **layer 2 scaling solution**.

A Deep Dive into Optimistic and ZK-Rollups
------------------------------------------

While many types of **layer 2 scaling solutions** exist, Rollups have really taken over as the dominant force. Think of them as the high-capacity carpools for the blockchain world. But not all carpools operate the same way.

The two main players, **Optimistic Rollups** and **ZK-Rollups**, take fundamentally different routes to achieve the same goal: faster, cheaper transactions. Getting a handle on their distinct mechanics is the key to understanding their trade-offs. One prioritizes efficiency with a trust-based model, while the other leans on some pretty advanced math for instant, verifiable proof.

![Image 5: Image](https://cdn.outrank.so/3af56023-0d2e-4234-a574-74a70915453f/abe538bf-1599-404d-b42f-6b0fb1541f14.jpg)
### Optimistic Rollups: The “Trust But Verify” Model

Optimistic Rollups operate on a simple, yet effective premise: they assume every transaction batch is valid by default. It's basically an honor system. The carpool driver (the sequencer) bundles all the trips and tells the main highway authority (Layer 1) that everything is correct, without showing proof upfront.

This "innocent until proven guilty" approach is incredibly efficient. It sidesteps the heavy computational work of generating complex proofs for every single batch, which helps keep fees low and the whole system moving quickly.

Of course, this optimism needs a safety net. After a batch is submitted to Layer 1, there's a **challenge period**, which usually lasts about seven days. During this window, anyone watching the network (a verifier) can jump in and dispute the batch if they spot something fraudulent. If a challenge succeeds, the faulty transaction gets reversed, and the bad actor is penalized.

This mechanism has a direct impact on users:

*   **Withdrawal Times:** That challenge period means you have to wait for it to pass before you can move your funds back to the Layer 1 mainnet. It’s a crucial security feature to make sure nobody can run off with stolen funds.
*   **Simplicity:** Optimistic Rollups are generally more compatible with the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM), making it much easier for developers to bring their existing dApps over.

### ZK-Rollups: The Mathematically Proven Model

In sharp contrast, ZK-Rollups (Zero-Knowledge Rollups) take a "trustless" approach. Instead of just assuming transactions are valid, they use advanced cryptography to _prove_ they are. Before any batch of transactions gets sent to Layer 1, the sequencer generates a cryptographic proof called a **validity proof**.

This proof mathematically confirms that every single transaction in the batch followed the rules, all without revealing the underlying data—hence the "zero-knowledge" name.

> A validity proof is like giving the highway authority a cryptographically sealed, tamper-proof document that instantly verifies the entire carpool trip was legitimate. There's no need for a challenge period because the validity is already proven with mathematical certainty.

This model comes with some major benefits:

*   **Faster Finality:** Since validity is proven right away, there's no need for a long challenge period. This means you can withdraw your funds back to Layer 1 almost instantly.
*   **Enhanced Privacy:** By their very nature, ZK proofs can obscure transaction details, offering a higher degree of confidentiality.
*   **Security Guarantees:** The system's integrity relies on hard math, not on the vigilance of network watchers, which provides incredibly robust security.

This kind of technology is a huge deal for overcoming the scalability issues that have held back Web3's mass adoption. By drastically lowering gas fees—sometimes to less than **$0.05** during busy times on networks like Arbitrum and zkSync—these solutions are making blockchain practical for everyday use.

### Optimistic Rollups vs. ZK-Rollups: A Practical Comparison

So, when it comes down to it, which one is better? The honest answer is: it depends. The choice between these two types of **layer 2 scaling solutions** involves a direct trade-off between speed, complexity, and cost. While both are designed to boost throughput, their operational differences make them better suited for different applications.

Let's break down the key distinctions.

| Feature | Optimistic Rollups | ZK-Rollups |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Verification Method** | Fraud Proofs (Challenge-based) | Validity Proofs (Cryptographic) |
| **Withdrawal Time** | Long (typically ~7 days) | Fast (minutes to hours) |
| **Transaction Fees** | Generally lower due to less computation | Can be higher due to proof generation |
| **EVM Compatibility** | High and more mature | Improving rapidly with ZK-EVMs |
| **Ideal Use Cases** | General DeFi, NFT trading, governance | Payments, exchanges, privacy apps |

Optimistic Rollups, with their battle-tested simplicity and EVM-friendliness, have been a popular choice for many DeFi protocols that need complex smart contract interactions. They just work.

On the other hand, ZK-Rollups are quickly gaining ground, especially as the technology becomes more accessible. Their fast finality makes them perfect for applications where quick settlement is critical, like decentralized exchanges and payment systems. Sequencers on these networks also play a key role in transaction ordering, which can sometimes lead to opportunities for value extraction. To get a better handle on this, check out our guide on [what MEV is and how it works](https://thecryptorecruiters.io/what-is-mev/).

While Rollups get most of the attention in the blockchain scaling world, they’re far from the only game in town. Other powerful **layer 2 scaling solutions**, like Sidechains and State Channels, fill specific, vital roles that are a perfect fit for certain types of applications.

Knowing the difference is key. Instead of bundling transactions like a Rollup, Sidechains and State Channels are built on entirely different ideas. One gives you the flexibility of a whole new blockchain, while the other offers blistering speed for a very narrow kind of interaction. Picking the wrong one can lead to security headaches or a clunky, inefficient design.

### When to Build on a Sidechain

Think of a Sidechain as a separate, independent blockchain that runs alongside a mainnet like Ethereum. It's hooked up to the mainnet with a two-way bridge, letting you move assets back and forth. But here's the catch: it operates with its own rules, its own consensus mechanism (like Proof of Stake), and its own group of validators. This independence is both its biggest strength and its main trade-off.

A great example is the [Polygon PoS](https://polygon.technology/polygon-pos) chain. It’s a full-blown ecosystem where developers can build complex dApps, launch massive gaming worlds, or create lively communities with all the smart contract features they need. The crucial thing to remember is that **its security is separate from Ethereum's**. You have to trust that the Sidechain’s own validators are doing their job.

So, when does a Sidechain make sense?

*   **You need total flexibility and control:** Sidechains let you design custom governance models and tokenomics that just aren't possible on a more constrained Layer 2.
*   **Your app is a self-contained world:** If you're building a huge game or a platform that needs its own dedicated blockspace without competing with others, a Sidechain is a fantastic choice.
*   **You're okay with a different security model:** The project and its users need to be comfortable trusting the Sidechain's own validator set, rather than relying on the security of the Layer 1 mainnet.

### When to Use a State Channel

Now, let's switch gears completely and look at State Channels. These are built for a much more specific job. Imagine you and a friend are playing a card game where you trade a tiny bit of crypto with every hand. Putting every single one of those little transactions on a public blockchain would be painfully slow and incredibly expensive.

> A State Channel is like opening a private, off-chain tab between two or more people. You can make a virtually unlimited number of transactions between yourselves, instantly and with zero gas fees. The only things that hit the main blockchain are the initial setup and the final settlement.

This design makes State Channels the most efficient tool out there for high-frequency, peer-to-peer interactions.

Their use case is niche, but they're incredibly powerful within that niche. State Channels are the perfect solution for:

*   **In-game micropayments** where players are constantly swapping small assets.
*   **Peer-to-peer video or data streaming services** that might bill you by the second.
*   **Recurring subscription payments** between two specific parties.

The big limitation? They aren't built for general use. They only work for applications with a fixed group of participants who need to interact with each other over and over again. They can't support the kind of open, public activity that a dApp on a Sidechain or Rollup can handle.

How Layer 2s Are Powering Real-World Applications
-------------------------------------------------

The theory behind **layer 2 scaling solutions** is one thing, but their real magic shines when you see them in action. These aren't just concepts on a whiteboard anymore; they're the engine making Web3 practical and usable for millions of people today. By shifting activity away from congested mainnets, they're unlocking a world of new possibilities in finance, gaming, and social media.

![Image 6: Image](https://cdn.outrank.so/3af56023-0d2e-4234-a574-74a70915453f/2b3f6eaf-9a09-41fb-a543-fc7260fe0acd.jpg)
From decentralized exchanges to digital collectibles, Layer 2s are delivering the speed and low costs that mainstream adoption has been waiting for.

### Fueling the DeFi Revolution

Decentralized Finance (DeFi) was one of the first sectors to feel the crippling effects of Layer 1 congestion. On Ethereum, soaring gas fees made simple token swaps or lending activities ridiculously expensive for the average person.

This is where Optimistic Rollups like **Arbitrum** and **Optimism** completely changed the game. DeFi protocols built on these Layer 2s offer a trading experience that’s both lightning-fast and shockingly cheap, often costing just a few cents per transaction.

Now, users can trade, lend, and farm yield without constantly worrying about gas fees eating away their profits. It has democratized access to sophisticated financial tools that were once only practical for whale-sized traders.

### Redefining Gaming and NFTs

The world of blockchain gaming and NFTs has also been supercharged by Layer 2 tech. Minting a new NFT or trading in-game items on Layer 1 could easily cost more than the asset itself, creating a massive roadblock for players and creators.

Platforms like **Immutable X**, a ZK-Rollup built specifically for gaming, have wiped this problem out by offering **zero gas fees** for minting and trading NFTs. This has ignited a new economy where players can truly own and trade their digital assets freely and frequently. It's a fundamental shift that makes complex game economies and massive NFT collections finally feasible.

> By moving these high-volume, low-value transactions to a dedicated Layer 2, developers can build far more engaging and interactive experiences without the economic friction of a mainnet.

This move to faster, cheaper layers is making a huge impact. In 2025, Layer 2s have become a vital growth engine for Ethereum, pumping up the network's overall activity by an estimated **20%**. In fact, solutions like Base and Optimism often blow past Ethereum’s Layer 1 in daily active users and transaction counts, proving that these scaling solutions are where the real action is happening.

### Enabling the Next Wave of Social Apps

Even social media is getting a Web3 upgrade, all thanks to **layer 2 scaling solutions**. Platforms built on networks like **Base** are making seamless micro-transactions and new kinds of user interactions possible in a way that would be unimaginable on a clogged mainnet.

Imagine tipping a creator a fraction of a penny for their content or earning rewards for your engagement in real-time. These are the kinds of features that become a reality when transaction costs are practically zero.

This opens the door for entirely new business models for creators and more rewarding experiences for users. The demand for talent to build these applications is exploding, and many companies now need to [hire blockchain developers](https://thecryptorecruiters.io/hire-blockchain-developers/) with specific Layer 2 expertise. These real-world examples show that Layer 2s aren't just a "nice to have"—they're the critical infrastructure making Web3 a reality.

The Future of Blockchain Scaling and Interoperability
-----------------------------------------------------

The evolution of **layer 2 scaling solutions** is far from over. As we look ahead, the conversation is shifting from just scaling individual networks to building a deeply interconnected and efficient Web3 ecosystem that can finally support billions of users.

The path forward really boils down to two key ideas: **modularity** and **interoperability**. We're moving beyond the old "one chain does it all" model and stepping into the era of modular blockchains.

### The Rise of Modular Blockchains

So, what is a modular blockchain? Think of it like a modern assembly line, where each station has a highly specialized job and does it incredibly well. Instead of one chain handling everything, a modular design separates the core functions:

*   **Execution** (running the apps)
*   **Data Availability** (storing transaction data)
*   **Settlement** (finalizing the transactions)

This approach unlocks incredible customization and efficiency. A Layer 2 network can focus on lightning-fast execution while leaning on a separate, dedicated data layer and settling its final state on a super-secure mainnet like Ethereum.

This specialization means each component can be fine-tuned for its specific task, creating a system that’s way more powerful than the sum of its parts.

> By unbundling these core functions, developers can build highly specialized and performant applications without compromising on decentralization or security. It’s the ultimate "have your cake and eat it too" scenario for blockchain architecture.

### Seamless Interoperability and What’s Next

The other major frontier is **interoperability**—making sure assets and information can move freely between different Layer 2s. The future isn't about a single "winner-take-all" Layer 2. It’s about a vibrant, buzzing ecosystem of many chains all working together.

New protocols are popping up to create trustless bridges, aiming to make moving assets between, say, [Arbitrum](https://arbitrum.io/) and a ZK-Rollup as seamless as switching between websites today.

Fueling this progress are game-changing innovations like **ZK-EVMs** (Zero-Knowledge Ethereum Virtual Machines). These are a huge deal. They make the powerful security and speed of ZK-Rollups much easier for developers to actually use. By letting developers deploy their existing Ethereum smart contracts directly onto a ZK-Rollup, ZK-EVMs slash the barrier to entry and will massively speed up the adoption of this advanced tech.

Ultimately, these breakthroughs show that **layer 2 scaling solutions** aren't just a temporary fix. They're the foundational building blocks for a truly scalable, multi-chain future, paving the way for a more connected and accessible internet for everyone.

Got Questions About Layer 2?
----------------------------

As you dive into the world of **layer 2 scaling**, a few questions always seem to pop up. Getting your head around the answers is key to understanding how these powerful networks deliver their impressive speed and low costs—and what trade-offs they make along the way.

Let’s tackle some of the most common ones.

### Are Layer 2s as Secure as Layer 1s?

This is the big one, and the honest answer is: it depends on the type of Layer 2.

Rollups, whether they're Optimistic or ZK, are designed from the ground up to borrow the full security of the Layer 1 blockchain they settle on, like Ethereum. Think of them as extensions that are securely tethered to the mainnet, inheriting its protections.

Sidechains are a different story. They run their own show with independent consensus mechanisms and validator sets. This gives them a ton of flexibility, but it also means their security is entirely separate from the mainnet. When you use a sidechain, you’re trusting its security model, not Ethereum's. State Channels are super secure, but only for the specific parties involved within that private channel.

> When maximum, mainnet-level security is a deal-breaker, **Rollups are the undisputed best choice**.

### If Layer 2 Is Cheaper and Faster, Why Bother with Layer 1?

Layer 1 is, and always will be, the ultimate source of truth for the entire ecosystem. It’s the bedrock, the supreme court of the blockchain world. While Layer 2s handle the fast and cheap daily grind of transactions, the Layer 1 mainnet is where everything is ultimately finalized and secured.

It plays a few irreplaceable roles:

*   **The Final Word:** It’s the final, indisputable ledger where all activity is recorded for good.
*   **The Judge:** It's the courtroom where disputes from Optimistic Rollups are settled using fraud proofs.
*   **The Security Anchor:** It provides the hardcore cryptographic security that the whole Layer 2 ecosystem is built on.

So, while most of us will be spending our time on Layer 2s for a smoother experience, Layer 1 remains the indispensable anchor of trust holding it all together.

### How Do I Move My Crypto to a Layer 2?

Getting your assets from a Layer 1 to a Layer 2 is done using a tool called a **bridge**. It’s basically a set of smart contracts that locks your tokens (like ETH or USDC) on the mainnet and then "mints" an identical, wrapped version of them on the Layer 2 network.

The process is usually pretty simple. Major networks like [Arbitrum](https://bridge.arbitrum.io/), [Optimism](https://app.optimism.io/bridge/deposit), and [Base](https://bridge.base.org/) all have official, user-friendly bridge interfaces right on their websites.

When you want to bring your funds back to Layer 1, you just use the bridge again to reverse the process. One thing to keep in mind, though: withdrawing from Optimistic Rollups comes with a waiting period, typically around **seven days**, which is a built-in security feature.

Layer-1 and Layer-2 Blockchain Scaling Solutions
================================================

There are two primary ways to achieve blockchain scalability: Layer-1 and Layer-2 solutions.

By [Cryptopedia Staff](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/authors#cryptopedia-staff)

Updated February 26, 2025 • 5 min read

[![Image 5: share with twitter](https://www.gemini.com/_next/static/media/cryptopedia_social_twitter.19a72290.svg)](https://twitter.com/share?url=https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-layer-2-network-layer-1-network)[![Image 6: share with linkedin](https://www.gemini.com/_next/static/media/cryptopedia_social_linkedin.5d02d7b5.svg)](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-layer-2-network-layer-1-network)[![Image 7: share with facebook](https://www.gemini.com/_next/static/media/cryptopedia_social_facebook.c161a6a1.svg)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-layer-2-network-layer-1-network)

![Image 8: Layer-1 and Layer-2 Blockchain Scaling Solutions-100](https://images.ctfassets.net/0idwgenf7ije/73NRF13FJwiwM0ULAdtDAv/d93b3a9e54181df6e2e5bfe0b143f282/Layer-1_and_Layer-2_Blockchain_Scaling_Solutions-100.jpg?fm=webp&w=1024&q=100)

#### Summary

To compete with legacy systems of payment processing, blockchain networks must become highly scalable — capable of accommodating an exponentially growing number of users, transactions, and data. Only by adequately incorporating scalability into their structure do blockchain networks stand to supersede other legacy systems. Layer-1 solutions add utility to a native blockchain to optimize its performance. Layer-2 solutions are third-party protocols that integrate with an underlying Layer-1 blockchain to increase transactional throughput.

What Is Blockchain Scalability?
-------------------------------

While [blockchain technology](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-technology-explained) is proving itself to be a new pillar of the global economy, its underlying structure of [decentralized networks](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-network-decentralized-distributed-centralized) faces a unique challenge known as the [Blockchain Trilemma](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/blockchain-trilemma-decentralization-scalability-definition): the balancing act between decentralization, security, and scalability within a blockchain infrastructure.

Blockchain decentralization refers to the meaningful distribution of computing power and consensus throughout a network, while security reflects a blockchain protocol’s defenses against malicious actors and network attacks. Both are considered non-negotiable to the function of a blockchain network.

Also essential is scalability, which refers to a blockchain network’s ability to support high transactional [throughput](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#throughput) and future growth. Scalability is crucial because it represents the only way for blockchain networks to reasonably compete with [legacy](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#legacy-system), [centralized](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#centralization) platforms with rapid settlement times. A commonly used comparison to indicate the gulf in scalability is that [Bitcoin](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/how-does-bitcoin-mining-work) processes between 4–7 transactions per second (TPS). Visa, on the other hand, processes thousands of TPS. In order to compete with these existing systems, blockchain technology must match or exceed these high levels of scalability. There now exists an entire sub-sector of the blockchain industry that’s working towards improving scalability.

Thankfully, a whole new generation of blockchains and [scaling solutions](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#scaling-solution) built specifically to solve this transaction-capacity problem is exponentially increasing the scaling limits of blockchain and making meaningful progress. These projects address scalability in two different ways: [Layer-1](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#layer-1-blockchain) and [Layer-2 scaling solutions](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#layer-2-scaling-solution).

Layer-1 Scaling Solutions
-------------------------

In the decentralized ecosystem, a Layer-1 network refers to a blockchain, while a Layer-2 protocol is a third-party integration that can be used in conjunction with a Layer-1 blockchain. Bitcoin, [Litecoin](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/litecoin-vs-bitcoin-blockchain), and [Ethereum](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/ethereum-blockchain-smart-contracts-dapps), for example, are Layer-1 blockchains. Layer-1 scaling solutions augment the base layer of the blockchain protocol itself in order to improve scalability. A number of methodologies are currently being developed — and practiced — that improve the scalability of blockchain networks directly.

Here’s how it works: Layer-1 solutions change the rules of the protocol directly to increase transaction capacity and speed, while accommodating more users and data. Layer-1 scaling solutions can entail, for example, increasing the amount of data contained in each [block](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#block), or accelerating the rate at which blocks are confirmed, so as to increase overall network throughput.

Other foundational updates to a blockchain to achieve Layer-1 network scaling include:

Consensus protocol improvements: Some [consensus mechanisms](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#consensus-mechanism) are more efficient than others. [Proof of Work (PoW)](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#proof-of-work-po-w) is the consensus protocol currently in use on popular blockchain networks like Bitcoin. Although PoW is secure, it can be slow. That’s why many newer blockchain networks favor the [Proof-of-Stake (PoS)](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/what-is-staking-crypto-proof-of-stake-pos-blockchains) consensus mechanism. Instead of requiring [miners](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#miner) to solve cryptographic algorithms using substantial computing power, PoS systems process and validate new blocks of transaction data based on participants staking collateral in the network.

With [Ethereum 2.0](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/ethereum-blockchain-pos-proof-of-stake), Ethereum will transition to a PoS consensus algorithm, which is expected to dramatically and fundamentally increase the capacity of the Ethereum network while increasing decentralization and preserving network security.

Sharding: Sharding is a mechanism adapted from distributed databases that has become one of the most popular Layer-1 scaling solutions, despite its somewhat experimental nature within the blockchain sector. Sharding entails breaking the [state](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#state) of the entire blockchain network into distinct datasets called "shards" — a more manageable task than requiring all nodes to maintain the entire network. These network shards are simultaneously processed in parallel by the network, allowing for sequential work on numerous transactions.

Further, each network node is assigned to a particular shard instead of maintaining a copy of the blockchain in its entirety. Individual shards provide proofs to the [mainchain](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#mainchain) and interact with one another to share addresses, balances, and general states using cross-shard communication protocols. Ethereum 2.0 is one high-profile blockchain protocol that is exploring shards, along with [Zilliqa](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/zilliqa-coin-staking-zil-crypto), [Tezos](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/tezos-xtz-coin-digital-commonwealth), and [Qtum](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/qtum-crypto-and-blockchain-evm).

Layer-2 Scaling Solutions
-------------------------

Layer-2 refers to a network or technology that operates on top of an underlying blockchain protocol to improve its scalability and efficiency. This category of scaling solutions entails shifting a portion of a blockchain protocol’s transactional burden to an adjacent system architecture, which then handles the brunt of the network’s processing and only subsequently reports back to the main blockchain to finalize its results. By abstracting the majority of data processing to auxiliary architecture, the base layer blockchain becomes less congested — and ultimately more scalable.

For instance, Bitcoin is a Layer-1 network, and the [Lightning Network](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/bitcoin-lightning-network-scaling-solution) is a Layer-2 solution built to improve transaction speeds in this fashion on the Bitcoin network. Other examples of Layer-2 solutions include:

Nested blockchains: A nested blockchain is essentially a blockchain within — or, rather, atop — another blockchain. The nested blockchain architecture typically involves a main blockchain that sets parameters for a broader network, while executions are undertaken on an interconnected web of secondary chains. Multiple blockchain levels can be built upon a mainchain, with each level using a parent-child connection. The parent chain delegates work to child chains that process and return it to the parent after completion. The underlying base blockchain does not take part in the network functions of secondary chains unless dispute resolution is necessary.

The distribution of work under this model reduces the processing burden on the mainchain to exponentially improve scalability. The [OMG Plasma project](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/what-is-omg-network) is an example of Layer-2 nested blockchain infrastructure that is utilized atop the Layer-1 Ethereum protocol to facilitate faster and cheaper transactions.

State channels:A state channel facilitates two-way communication between a blockchain and [off-chain](https://www.gemini.com/cryptopedia/glossary#off-chain) transactional channels and improves overall transaction capacity and speed. A state channel does not require validation by nodes of the Layer-1 network. Instead, it is a network-adjacent resource that is sealed off by using a multi-signature or smart contract mechanism. When a transaction or batch of transactions is completed on a state channel, the final “state” of the “channel” and all its inherent transitions are recorded to the underlying blockchain. The Liquid Network, Celer, Bitcoin Lightning, and Ethereum's Raiden Network are examples of state channels. In the Blockchain Trilemma tradeoff, state channels sacrifice some degree of decentralization to achieve greater scalability.

Sidechains: A sidechain is a blockchain-adjacent transactional chain that’s typically used for large batch transactions. Sidechains use an independent consensus mechanism — i.e., separate from the original chain — which can be optimized for speed and scalability. With a sidechain architecture, the primary role of the mainchain is to maintain overall security, confirm batched transaction records, and resolve disputes. Sidechains are differentiated from state channels in a number of integral ways. Firstly, sidechain transactions aren’t private between participants — they are publicly recorded to the ledger. Further, sidechain security breaches do not impact the mainchain or other sidechains. Establishing a sidechain might require substantial effort, as the infrastructure is usually built from the ground up.

Boosting Blockchain Network Scalability
---------------------------------------

Layer-1 and Layer-2 scaling solutions are two sides of the same crypto coin: They’re strategies designed to make blockchain networks faster and more accommodating to a rapidly growing user base. These strategies are not mutually exclusive either, and many blockchain networks are exploring combinations of Layer-1 and Layer-2 scaling solutions to achieve increased scalability without sacrificing adequate security or decentralization.

URL: https://www.starknet.io/blog/layer-2-scaling-solutions/

SUMMARY:
They work with L1 blockchains to process more transactions at lower costs, while still relying on the base layer’s security and decentralization. Layer 2 solutions reduce this strain by moving transaction processing away from the main chain while preserving the decentralized and trustless environment that blockchains are built on. Rollups are Layer 2 scaling solutions that shift the computational load of transactions offchain while keeping the main chain responsible for validation and security. L1 chains could act as secure settlement layers, ensuring decentralization and security while leaving transaction execution and user-facing operations to faster and cheaper L2 scaling solutions. Layer 2 (L2) scaling solutions are technologies designed to improve blockchain efficiency and performance without compromising security or decentralization.
